text:
Had the pleasure of joining ANI to share my latest views on the oil markets. Have a watch!

#oott

On Bloomberg TV today with Brent at $100/bbl. Watch from the 17th minute.

https://t.co/wq38JznlCq https://t.co/IkJoFbkZ2t

Fresh Houthi announcement, which muddles the waters a bit.

Paraphrasing: No closure of the Bab el-Mandeb strait; blockade only targets "the Saudi side," but that leaves unclear whether it includes (or not) foreign vessels lifting Saudi crude (or only Saudi oil tankers).

A mixed bag in the Red Sea: several tankers have U-turned and some Asian refiners are already making arrangements to pick up Saudi crude in the Mediterranean. But also a few other oil tankers have crossed the Bab el-Mandeb unharmed, and more are heading toward the chokepoint.

According to Kpler's data, we averaged 1 VLCC a day from July 19 to July 23, ~2 million b/d. 

Dark transit, so the US is still trying, which means more tanker attacks from the IRGC.

In an interview with the newspaper, The National, on 8 July 2026, when talking about the Strait of Hormuz situation, Dr Fatih Birol, the Executive Director of the International Energy Agency (IEA), said that Gulf oil exporters “need to regain their reputation as reliable exporters.”

This description of producers amounts to a value judgement. Gulf producers, according to Dr Birol, are no longer trustworthy suppliers of oil. It is a statement that makes for an easy headline, but one that has several flaws.

OPEC does not dismiss the challenges related to a full reopening of the Strait. That said, others should not dismiss historical context and evidence with rhetoric and sweeping statements. This perspective matters.
#oott

BP nears deal to sell solar business Lightsource to Kuwait-backed group
Qualitas Energy and investment arm of sovereign wealth fund in advanced talks to purchase renewables business https://t.co/cNGCfWkjHF
Tankers carrying Saudi oil cross Bab al-Mandeb Strait despite Houthi threats, data shows #oott https://t.co/kGY6eHof0D

Friday gonna Friday.

Does Brent finish the week above or below $100?

Place your bets. https://t.co/9ndmFxss5l

(longer than I planned)

The question imho isn't whether or not Trump *can* TACO on Iran—when push comes to shove I think Trump can still choose to hard-bail and leave everyone else with the mess, what I would have called the "unilateral TACO" a few months ago.

Question is at what level of market stress—crude/pump price, equity market rout, etc.—will force Trump to do so?

I always thought that the now-dead MOU read a lot like the deal I expected Trump to be forced to make at $150+ crude, and it was strange to me that Trump agreed to it at like $80. 

But clearly Trump never intended to comply with the key provisions that got Tehran to agree to reopen the Strait, which makes a lot more sense at $80. Hence why the deal—which was fundamentally unstable given competing interpretations of those key provisions—collapsed and we're back to a tightening oil market and a rising price trajectory in an attempt to find the TACO clearing price.

There's also always a possibility that Iran eventually taps out first, but I think so far Tehran seems intent on leveraging the Hormuz crisis while they're got it.

The month-long post-MOU partial reopening kind of helped give the market a temporary supply hit, but most of that was just the transfer of stranded oil-on-water in the Gulf into the broader system. Fresh Gulf loadings, meanwhile, failed to durably rebound and are falling again.

Meanwhile global commercial stock levels have continued to draw, SPRs are lower, and now we have a deepening refined product crisis on top of Hormuz thanks to Russia's Ukraine-assisted refinery fleet implosion.

So oil market is now caught between more durably tighter balances and not wanting to get rugged on the upside in an inevitable replay of prior TACO jawbones/head fakes. 

Finally, there's still that pesky presumed surplus on the other side of this crisis, which makes it easier all else equal to tolerate low stock levels (we can just refill them in the glut! right?!). But future barrels won't matter if we *actually* start getting close to local tank bottoms, which would force prices sharply higher to balance as patience is exhausted.

It's a race!

Fascinating market, hard to believe I'll ever see another one like it.

A cleaned up and expanded version of this will likely make it into today's Oil Context Weekly report.

Check out last week's report below and subscribe!

I am waiting for the salvo of apocalyptic forecasts like $150/200 per barrel during current US/Iran confrontation as my entry point to short crude oil #oott #crudeoil
